caveats: a: BLS occupation data cover all motor vehicle manufacturing, not a verified LMM contract subset.
a: ILO exposure is broad and measures technical potential rather than current unautomated work.
a: The dataset labor ratio is measured at NAICS 3361 and may not describe a hypothetical small-firm lens.

rho: Large-OEM implementation experience may not transfer to LMM firms.

e: No replacement for the frozen missing firm count was researched or estimated.

s5: The absence of a defensible population makes economy-wide owner-age evidence uninformative here.

q: Commercial terms may differ materially between complete-vehicle and chassis-only programs.

d5: Monthly assembly rates are cyclical and can be distorted by shutdowns and model changeovers.
d5: EIA scenarios depend materially on policy assumptions.

o: OEM insourcing could eliminate outsourced demand even while vehicle demand remains.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Automobile and Light Duty Motor Vehicle Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Multi-period B2B contract-manufacturing programs with tooling, qualification, launch, assembly, testing, and delivery obligations; revenue would derive from program units, engineering changes, and associated manufacturing support rather than retail vehicle branding.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to outsourced contract vehicle or chassis manufacturing because the NAICS is dominated by proprietary OEM product manufacturing, while the fixed screen requires repeat outsourced service. Public evidence did not establish a defensible LMM target population within that subset.
lens.excluded_activities: Large proprietary vehicle OEMs, captive assembly plants, non-control financing vehicles, motor-vehicle body builders using purchased chassis, motor-home manufacturers using purchased chassis, race-car manufacturers, component suppliers, dealers, and one-off prototype work without repeat production.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, providing repeat outsourced contract manufacturing of complete automobiles, light-duty motor vehicles, or chassis to external vehicle brands or program owners.

a.bridge: The BLS occupation mix for motor vehicle manufacturing, led by 152,670 miscellaneous assemblers and fabricators in May 2024, is combined with ILO broad-occupation GenAI exposure to infer wage-weighted current opportunity for the hypothetical contract-manufacturing lens. This may overstate exposure if small contract plants remain highly physical and understate it if engineering, launch, and documentation roles carry higher wages.
caveats: BLS occupation data cover all motor vehicle manufacturing, not a verified LMM contract subset.
ILO exposure is broad and measures technical potential rather than current unautomated work.
The dataset labor ratio is measured at NAICS 3361 and may not describe a hypothetical small-firm lens.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted staffing roster and task-time study for actual LMM contract vehicle manufacturers, including current robotics and software baselines, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can assist engineering change analysis, production planning, quality documentation, maintenance knowledge, procurement, scheduling, and administrative work, but final assembly, materials movement, paint, machining, and physical inspection dominate employment.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve assembly volumes, BLS sectoral output for NAICS 3361, and EIA light-duty powertrain scenarios are translated into year-five constant-quality demand for outsourced contract assembly. This likely overstates the lens if volume remains concentrated in integrated OEM plants and could understate it if new entrants outsource launches or flexible EV production.
caveats: Industry output and assembly volumes measure all OEM production, not outsourced LMM programs.
Monthly assembly rates are cyclical and can be distorted by shutdowns and model changeovers.
EIA scenarios depend materially on policy assumptions.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year production forecasts by plant and brand, contract-manufacturing awards, launch schedules, capacity utilization, cancellation rates, and constant-price unit economics would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: U.S. auto and light-truck assemblies were 10.273 million at an annual rate in May 2026 versus 10.753 million a year earlier, while BLS sectoral output eased from 2024 to 2025. Replacement demand persists, but powertrain-policy scenarios and domestic sourcing decisions create wide uncertainty.

caveats: The NAICS definition identifies activities, not firm revenue models or size.
No replacement for the frozen missing firm count was researched or estimated.
e.change_evidence: A verified establishment-to-firm roster with ownership, EBITDA, captive status, brand ownership, contract-manufacturing revenue, customer recurrence, and control eligibility is required.
e.rationale: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, and public NAICS sources do not identify which firms, if any, are independent repeat contract manufacturers rather than proprietary OEMs or captive plants. A denominator and eligible numerator therefore cannot be bounded honestly.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 3361: labor compensation (L02) $37,378.5M / sectoral output (T30) $457,278.3M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $1,594,777 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 33611); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate addresses elimination or self-service, not conventional robotics already embedded in production.
OEM insourcing could eliminate outsourced demand even while vehicle demand remains.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of OEM insourcing, radically modular self-assembly, autonomous dark factories without transferable operating organizations, or product substitution eliminating complete-vehicle production would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Complete vehicles and chassis remain physical, regulated products requiring an accountable manufacturer for assembly, validation, traceability, recalls, and warranty interfaces. AI can change workflows and vehicle technology but does not remove the manufacturing operator.

caveats: No public contract sample for a verified U.S. LMM contract-vehicle-manufacturing population was found.
Commercial terms may differ materially between complete-vehicle and chassis-only programs.
q.change_evidence: Actual manufacturing agreements, open-book provisions, annual cost-down clauses, program duration, change-order economics, renewal outcomes, and customer concentration would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: A contract manufacturer could retain gains during fixed-price program periods, but vehicle customers typically possess strong purchasing leverage, open-book costing, productivity givebacks, annual price reductions, and rebid rights that force substantial sharing.

caveats: No AI implementation study for the screened contract-manufacturing population was found.
Large-OEM implementation experience may not transfer to LMM firms.
rho.change_evidence: Measured production pilots reporting adoption, defects, downtime, cycle time, headcount avoidance, validation cost, worker acceptance, and customer approval would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Planning, documentation, knowledge retrieval, procurement, quality analytics, and engineering support can be implemented within five years, but safety, unions, product liability, validation, cybersecurity, legacy systems, and tightly coupled production processes constrain deployment.

caveats: Large OEM transactions do not proxy LMM owner succession.
The absence of a defensible population makes economy-wide owner-age evidence uninformative here.
s5.change_evidence: After establishing the eligible roster, target owner age, shareholder structure, succession plans, sponsor ownership, broker mandates, and five-year control-transaction history would be required.
s5.rationale: No defensible eligible LMM firm population was identified, so a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability for that population cannot be estimated without inventing targets or applying an unrelated owner-age proxy.

principal_driver: Persistent physical demand for automobiles and light trucks, with potential episodic outsourcing around new programs or flexible capacity.
principal_weakness: No defensible LMM contract-manufacturer population or five-year transfer probability can be established from available evidence.
